commentaries.author: Bede
commentaries.content: cup: The golden cup full of abominations is hypocrisy, because hypocrites "outwardly appear righteous unto men, but within are full of all uncleanness." <a

commentaries.author: Bede
commentaries.content: gilded: That is, with all the allurements of simulated truth. Lastly, he explains what is within this beauteousness, as follows.

commentaries.author: Bede
commentaries.content: purple: In purple is shewn the false appearance of simulated rule, in scarlet the blood-stained dress of ungodliness.

commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content: Cup. Full of the abomination These are common scriptural expressions for the abominations of idolatry, with which ancient Rome had notoriously polluted herself. For not content with worshipping her own heathenish gods, she adopted those of all the countries and nations she had subdued. In Rome itself there were no less than 420 heathenish temples, so that one of their most famous poets, Ovid, (lib. i. Trist.) says: Sed quæ de septem totum circumspicit orbem, Monti bus Imperii, Roma, Deûmque locus.

methodology.step_1: Parse main page with BeautifulSoup for structure
methodology.step_3: Use Firecrawl to get full content from 'Go to Commentary' links
methodology.step_4: Fallback to BeautifulSoup for accordion content if needed
advantages: Records verses even without commentaries for complete tracking
